sourceMovie.title: Poltergeist
sourceMovie.whyPeopleLoveIt: Poltergeist works as a horror movie with a point of view—fans quote moments, but they also argue about what the film is actually saying about power, desire, or survival. The film leans hard into its core genre, which is exactly what fans say they wanted more of from blockbusters in that era.

recommendations.title: Psycho
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Psycho (1960) belongs to the same conversation as Poltergeist — both anchor their horror in thriller emotion that doesn't let go. The dark undercurrent connects them more than any plot summary would suggest.

recommendations.title: Hereditary
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: If Poltergeist's horror stayed with you, Hereditary (2018) delivers the same sensation through an entirely different story. The mystery through-line is the real common thread between them.

recommendations.title: Insidious
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Where Poltergeist excels in horror, Insidious (2011) doubles down on thriller to comparable effect. The tonal similarities run deep enough that watching both back-to-back feels intentional.

recommendations.title: The Babadook
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: The Babadook (2014) is the natural next step after Poltergeist: same horror DNA, different perspective. The dark streak and drama elevate it well above a simple genre exercise.

recommendations.title: A Nightmare on Elm Street
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Fans of Poltergeist who respond to its horror will find A Nightmare on Elm Street (1984) immediately familiar. Both films share a dark sensibility without ever feeling like carbon copies of each other.

recommendations.title: Silent Hill
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: There's a reason Poltergeist fans keep landing on Silent Hill (2006) — the horror is unmistakably related even when the plots diverge. Both films operate on the same emotional frequency, especially in their third acts.

recommendations.title: The Autopsy of Jane Doe
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: The Autopsy of Jane Doe draws from the same horror well as Poltergeist, channeling mystery in ways that feel unmistakably related. The 2016 film makes a natural companion watch — similar in spirit but distinctly its own.

recommendations.title: Insidious: Chapter 2
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Insidious: Chapter 2 (2013) captures the horror appeal of Poltergeist without simply imitating it. The thriller streak running through both films is what puts Insidious: Chapter 2 at the top of any "if you liked…" shortlist.

recommendations.title: Lights Out
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: The horror appeal of Poltergeist — that dark edge, the craft — translates cleanly into Lights Out (2016). Different setting, same essential satisfaction.

recommendations.title: Oculus
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: The filmmakers behind Oculus (2014) clearly understood what makes horror work — the same quality that keeps Poltergeist rewatchable. Expect the same dark rhythm, executed with a fresh angle.

faq.question: What should I watch after Poltergeist?
faq.answer: Try Psycho, Hereditary, Insidious—each shares DNA with Poltergeist while changing the setting enough to feel fresh.

faq.question: What genre is Poltergeist?
faq.answer: Poltergeist (1982) is best described as Horror. Stores and algorithms use different labels, but that mix is what fans actually recognize on a rewatch.

faq.question: Is Poltergeist part of a franchise?
faq.answer: Yes—it's part of the "Poltergeist Collection" storyline. You can enjoy it standalone, but sequels and tie-ins build on characters and continuity established here.
